So let me go back now to the days of strattin' and I'll make you laugh a little bit. We'll keep it clean, right? But in the movie, the wolf of Wall Street, right? Leo gives this amazing speech. Where he stands in front of all the strattin' and I said, this speech came literally word for word out of the book. It was like verbatim what I wrote in the book and I wrote the book myself. It was no ghost, right? So it was my own words and those words came directly from a very famous sales meeting I gave to everybody where I was, you know, one day I was on a roll and I started saying, because I was trying to make them laugh because I think people learn more when they laugh. But now remember that I had all these young kids, alright, and they were all about the same age. They mostly all come from disempowered backgrounds. And I had to speak to many people at once, right? So I was, you know, looking out at the crowd and I would say, guys, listen, here's the deal. You know, I said, if you don't want to get rich, you think I'm greedy or whatnot, then get the hell out of here, right? But before you leave, take a good look at the person sitting to your left, to your right, because one day in the not so distant future, you'll pull up to a red light and they will be in this beautiful, amazing, poor, chaffirari with a gorgeous wife or husband sitting next to them with two gorgeous kids in the background and in the back seat, right? Okay. And what kind of car will you be driving? A beat up crappy pinto that has like rust on the back and you'll have this terribly awful, hideous looking wife or husband. Now again, I know I'm older and wiser now. I want to be at least a little bit correct, but remember what I was speaking to. What was I really doing there? What I was doing. So there's two sides to the equation to answer your question. It's harnessing the motivating factors of pain and pleasure as we did in belief systems, very similar, but with a different outcome. So when I was looking at these guys, you know, when you motivate groups of people, what do you do as lowest common denominator? What's the lowest common denominator for all? Well, they all want that beautiful cars, live in beautiful homes, marry beautiful partners and live what we call the life, the life, the best table to restaurant, the house in the Hamptons, the children in the finest schools, vacationing in the south of France, retiring their parents, being able to retire themselves in a young age with pride and dignity and self-sufficiency. Right? That was what they all aspire to as a group, lowest common denominator. Right? On the flip side, what was their worst fear? Five years from now, if you don't succeed here, you're going to see the people who did it and you're going to be, oh my God, what did I do? Look at my life. I have the oldest, most beat up car. I hate my husband or wife. They hate me back. My kids are, you know, just the worst. I have nothing to look forward to with a home. I rent a piece of crap apartment. I can't even get into McDonald's. So what I did, I used the power of language to paint this incredibly toxic picture that represent their deepest fears, their deepest pain points as a group. And then I'd say, but if you say the words I teach you and you work your tail off, you'll become as powerful as the most powerful CEO. You will have the life, the greatest this, the greatest that. And I go on and paint the picture for them and what they'll get if they actually do that. So it's essentially harnessing these dual levers of pain and pleasure. Now, I wasn't thinking of it in those terms back then. Many years later, when I started actually teaching self development and I met Dr. Richard Bailey, the founder of NLP, right? He's laughing. You read my book because you hit it so on without knowing it. You intuitively had just exactly right. You did it exactly right. You took people who had low stats because I realized quickly that I had discovered such a powerful system for influence and it almost allowed people to become rich. It was a ticket to riches, right? So it was like, it was so clear with making them visually step into the life that they wanted. You'll have the most beautiful family, the biggest house, your retiree, your parents, you'll have prime dignity. You'll be the toast to all these things that represent an ultimate pleasure for them. And here's the thing. I didn't say it once. I said it again and again and again and again, twice a day, every day for five years. So that's the last part. So in the end, it's all about pain and pleasure. Guys, I didn't invent this. And frankly, no, I did Dr. Bambler, who was a brilliant guy. You know, I love him, right? Seriously, what is it? You know, to me, it's being alone, not taking care of my family. When I say alone being insignificant, not providing for those that I love, not giving value to people, living a small life. That's these ultimate pain, that small life, right? Versus ultimate pleasure, taking care of the people I love, my children, my friends, my parents, right? Providing massive value, being significant, helping others. That to me is like the light of my life. The thought of that makes me jump out of bed. Well, I'm going to use those things to help me motivate myself if I don't feel motivated. If I can't get myself to do what I got to do. Part of that is raising your standard.
